reasoning: The question asks if at least 10 countries will commit to *any* diplomatic agreement restricting solar geoengineering as a matter of "treaty obligation" before 2026. Reviewing recent evidence up to late October 2025, there is a growing global chorus, particularly from the 54 African countries represented in the AMCEN meeting in 2025, calling for a "Non-Use Agreement" for solar geoengineering. However, there is no actual binding treaty in place; it is purely in the proposal and political momentum phase. A 'treaty obligation' formally binding on 10 or more nations is not something that gets drafted, adopted, signed, and formally committed to within two months. Since the deadline is December 31, 2025, it is functionally impossible for a new treaty procedure to finalize with 10 countries making a binding obligation within this timeframe. Consequently, the community forecast at 2% is accurately reflecting the realities of international treaty law procedures. No concrete treaty constraint obligating states has passed as of late October 2025. I am therefore confident in a very low probability estimate of 2%.
